ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a bok choy food product entry; stir-fried preparation variant is not separately coded. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Bok choy (Chinese cabbage, pak choi) — FoodEx2 raw commodity anchor; stir-fried preparation not separately coded in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers bok choy as a raw vegetable commodity. No distinct stir-fried prepared dish code exists; closest parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for stir-fried bok choy as a prepared dish. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side dish serving of stir-fried bok choy is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a small bowl or side plate portion as commonly served in Singaporean Chinese households and hawker centres.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Bok Choy — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F14080037 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g core macro values are taken directly from the HPB record without modification. Saturated fat, potassium, calcium, and vitamin C are supplementary estimates from USDA FoodData Central (NDB 11740, cooked bok choy) consistent with the HPB macronutrient profile.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried bok choy in the University of Sydney GI Database. Bok choy is a non-starchy cruciferous vegetable with 1.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy with other non-starchy leafy vegetables (e.g., cabbage GI ~10, broccoli GI ~15) documented in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (15 × 1.6 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.36, rounded to 0.4. This is negligible and consistent with non-starchy vegetable classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium (181 mg/100 g) offset by potassium (~220 mg/100 g) and calcium (~80 mg/100 g); favourable Na:K ratio supports DASH dietary principles.
hypertension.guidance: Stir-fried bok choy is suitable for hypertensive patients when prepared with minimal soy sauce and oyster sauce; the naturally high potassium and calcium content supports vasodilation and blood pressure regulation consistent with DASH dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (1.6 g/100 g), minimal sugar (0.55 g/100 g), and estimated GI of 15 result in negligible glycaemic load (~0.4 per 150 g serving).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried bok choy is an excellent choice for type 2 diabetes management due to its negligible glycaemic impact; patients should limit added sauces containing sugar or starch to preserve the low-carbohydrate benefit.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.7 g/100 g), no trans fat, and sulforaphane from cruciferous bok choy provide anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective properties.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Stir-fried bok choy is cardioprotective due to its low saturated fat content, absence of trans fat, and cruciferous phytochemicals including sulforaphane and vitamin C (~30 mg/100 g); patients with CVD should request preparation with minimal oyster sauce to keep sodium within heart-healthy limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sulforaphane (anti-inflammatory), vitamin C (~30 mg/100 g), and calcium (~80 mg/100 g) are beneficial for RA patients, particularly those on corticosteroids.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Stir-fried bok choy is recommended for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its anti-inflammatory sulforaphane content and calcium support for corticosteroid-related bone protection; reduce soy sauce and oyster sauce to minimise sodium-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried bok choy is a classic Chinese-Singaporean vegetable side dish prepared by wok-frying fresh bok choy (Brassica rapa subsp. chinensis) with aromatic ingredients including garlic, ginger, and a small amount of soy sauce or oyster sauce in hot vegetable oil. The dish is characterised by tender-crisp stems, slightly wilted dark-green leaves, and a savoury, lightly garlicky flavour with a clean, fresh finish. It is a staple side dish in Singaporean Chinese households, hawker centres, and zi char restaurants, typically served alongside rice or noodle dishes. Nutritionally, stir-fried bok choy is low in calories (58 kcal/100 g), very low in carbohydrates (1.6 g/100 g), and provides moderate fat (4.9 g/100 g) primarily from cooking oil. It is a good source of vitamin C (~30 mg/100 g), calcium (~80 mg/100 g), and potassium (~220 mg/100 g), making it beneficial for bone health, immune function, and blood pressure regulation. The cruciferous nature of bok choy provides sulforaphane, a phytochemical with established anti-inflammatory and potential cardioprotective properties. Sodium content is moderate at 181 mg/100 g, largely attributable to soy sauce and oyster sauce used in preparation. The dish is suitable for vegetarians (when oyster sauce is omitted or substituted), halal diets, and is appropriate for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. Its negligible glycaemic load makes it an ideal vegetable choice for blood glucose management.

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14080037, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). The HPB record is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat, potassium, calcium, and vitamin C values are supplementary estimates drawn from USDA FoodData Central data for bok choy (NDB 11740, cooked) and are consistent with the HPB macronutrient profile. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008) as no direct GI measurement exists for this preparation.
notes: All core macros (protein_g 1.63, fat_g 4.9, carbohydrate_g 1.6) are non-zero and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo; no zero-macro justification is required. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because the data is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F14080037, and no sufficiently close match for the cooked stir-fried preparation with equivalent sauce additions was found in USDA FoodData Central: monounsaturated_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractionation not reported by HPB for this dish and not available in USDA FoodData Central NDB 11740 at the required preparation specificity; polyunsaturated_fat_g — same basis as monounsaturated_fat_g; trans_fat_g — not reported by HPB for this dish; iron_mg — micronutrient panel not reported by HPB for this dish and USDA cooked bok choy data (NDB 11740) does not match the sauce-added preparation; cholesterol_mg — not reported by HPB; vitamin_a_mcg_rae — not reported by HPB for this dish; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg — not reported by HPB for this dish; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg — not reported by HPB for this dish; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg — not reported by HPB for this dish; vitamin_b6_mg — not reported by HPB for this dish; vitamin_b12_mcg — not reported by HPB for this dish; folate_mcg_dfe — not reported by HPB for this dish; vitamin_d_mcg — not reported by HPB for this dish; vitamin_e_mg — not reported by HPB for this dish; phosphorus_mg — not reported by HPB for this dish; magnesium_mg — not reported by HPB for this dish; zinc_mg — not reported by HPB for this dish; selenium_mcg — not reported by HPB for this dish; water_g — moisture data for this specific stir-fried preparation not reported by HPB and not available at required preparation specificity in USDA FoodData Central. Sources consulted and confirmed unavailable for these fields: HPB Singapore Foodo (F14080037) and USDA FoodData Central NDB 11740 (bok choy, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt) as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat (0.7 g/100 g), potassium (~220 mg/100 g), calcium (~80 mg/100 g), and vitamin C (~30 mg/100 g) are supplementary estimates from USDA FoodData Central NDB 11740 consistent with the HPB macronutrient profile; these are marked as approximate. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on non-starchy leafy cruciferous vegetables (cabbage GI ~10, broccoli GI ~15) per Atkinson FS et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283 and the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for stir-fried bok choy exists in approved sources. Oyster sauce renders this dish non-vegan; a vegan variant using mushroom sauce or plain garlic oil would qualify as fully plant-based. The keto dietary tag from the input record is retained as the carbohydrate content (1.6 g/100 g) is consistent with ketogenic dietary thresholds.
